## [03:00 - 06:01]

And then the thing that I want to spend most time talking about here is ad optimization. Unlike setting up your accounts and unlike setting up your ads, there's no playbook for this. So having this is really going to help you understand how do you maximize your advertising dollars at all times. So it's broken into different sections. The top section here says automated testing options. Just know these are both optional, but there's two different ways that you can test on Facebook. The first one is dynamic creatives, which this is at done at the ad set level. And what it is is once you turn it on at the ad set level, it'll allow you to add multiple different variations. So you can put in different creatives, which means different videos or images, you can put in different headlines, different ad copy, you can actually even put in different call to action buttons. And what it's going to do for you is now that you've got all these different pieces in there is Facebook will just run all the variations, and it will spend the most money on the combinations that work the best. So say for example, you had two ad creatives, two headline copies, and in two ad copies, that would be two times two times two means that you would have eight variations of the ad, but you don't have to put in eight variations, you just have to put in two of each of those things, and then Facebook can turn it into eight variations. So the advantage of dynamic creative is that it's very simple to do. The downfall of it is that you don't really get all of the data in regards to which of the ad combinations works the best. And you can't really, you can't really pick and choose like, okay, let's keep this one on and let's turn these ones off. So it's, it's the advantages, it's simple. The disadvantages is that you don't have a lot of control over it. With a B split testing, it is a little bit more advanced. You turn the split test on at the campaign level, but then you can test many, many different kinds of variables. You can test entire campaigns against each other. You can test, you can test different ad sets, you can test different ads and different ad combinations. And while it's a little bit more of an advanced feature, it gives you the capability to say, for example, you've got an ad variation that works really well. It's like this image works great with this ad copy and this headline. Then what you can do is you can turn the other ones off. You can focus on that one and you can get as much activity into that singular ad as you want. So just know it is a little bit more advanced, but it does have that advantage to it. Okay, so when it comes to these two automated testing options, dynamic creatives and AV split tests, those are both things that you can kind of do as you get going. ## [12:11 - 15:15]

your Facebook ads to your websites and your click through rate will let you know what percentage of people who see that ad are clicking through. And that's going to help you know how compelling is your ad to click. The next thing to watch is frequency. So this is for any particular ad will show you how often that audience is seeing your ad. So in terms of what's the right frequency, I can tell you that most people probably think that anything over a frequency of two is too much. And the if if you're advertising like Grant Carton, I'm going to tell you right now, you're going to have a higher frequency than two. I've seen Grant have frequencies of three, four, five, six. But if you're seeing that the frequency is too high, maybe the solution for that is to create more ads. So just a very good thing to keep your eye on. Next one is CPM. CPM stands for cost per thousand, kind of a tricky one. But this is how much does it cost to serve up your ads? Remember that on Facebook, you're paying for exposures. So the CPM really is the only real metric that that matters in the eyes of how much is Facebook charging you. So this is going to tell you what it costs for the ads to get seen. And then things like cost per click, for example, are going to just be numbers that are derived from that CPM. Not only how they feel about your actual ads, the content every ads, but they also look at the content of the page that you're landing on. So if somebody leads your ad and goes to a page that's very spammy, it's going to hurt your quality ranking. So just keep an eye on this, because if you're, for example, your CPM, if your cost per 1000 exposures is too high, then there's a really good chance it's because your quality ranking isn't good. All of these things really work together. And then the last thing are additional rankings that Facebook creates, which are engagement and conversion ranking.
